---
title: "Claim graph"
subtitle: "The dependency graph and its commit-by-commit history"
toc: false
---

Every result in this project is a **claim** in a [Conventional Knowledge
Commits](https://conventional-knowledge-commits.org/) graph: a node whose colour is its
**effective status** (machine-checked, axiomatised, or open) and whose edges are the
`Depends-On` / `Closes` relations recorded in the commit trail. A claim's effective status is the
*weakest* of its own status and everything it rests on, so a single open leaf colours every result
above it -- the graph cannot flatter the work.

Click a node to trace what it depends on; press **Play** (or drag the slider) to replay the
commits in order and watch each claim's status resolve as the formalization advances. The view is
generated from git by [`claimgraph`](https://github.com/hotherio/claimgraph) and embedded below;
the same data drives the compact [history timeline](history.qmd).

<iframe src="claimgraph-viewer.html" title="ClaimGraph interactive viewer" loading="lazy"
        style="width:100%;height:820px;border:1px solid var(--bs-border-color,#dee2e6);border-radius:6px">
</iframe>

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The status colours are kernel-honest: they are derived from `#print axioms` over each node's Lean
name by `bin/axiom-report`, the same source that drives the [blueprint](proofs.qmd) badges. A node
is green only if its Lean compiles with no `sorry` and no project axiom.
:::
